questions.definition: A mode in the Claude desktop app that connects Claude to a folder on your computer. Unlike Chat (conversation only) or Projects (uploaded docs), Cowork lets Claude actually create, read, edit, and delete real files on your machine.
The folder on your computer that you link to a Cowork session. Claude can only see what is inside this folder and its subfolders. Everything else on your machine is invisible to it.
One continuous interaction with Claude in Cowork. When the session ends, Claude's temporary working memory is wiped. Only files saved to your workspace survive.

A markdown !!! file in your workspace that Claude reads automatically when a session starts. Think of it as the project's brain: it tells Claude what the project is about, what has been done, and how to behave.
Claude's temporary working area inside its sandbox. Files created here disappear when the session ends. Think of it as a whiteboard that gets erased.
The sandboxed computer environment Claude runs inside. It is isolated from the rest of your Mac and can only access your mounted workspace folder.
questions.section: How Claude Works Under the Hood

questions.definition: The bridge between Claude's sandbox and your real folder. When Claude saves a file to /mnt/, it appears in your workspace folder on disk.
questions.section: How Claude Works Under the Hood

questions.definition: When a conversation gets too long for Claude's memory, it automatically summarizes and trims earlier parts while trying to preserve key decisions.
questions.section: How Claude Works Under the Hood

questions.definition: The total amount of text Claude can hold in memory at once. When the conversation exceeds this, compaction happens.
questions.section: How Claude Works Under the Hood

questions.definition: Reusable capability modules that extend what Claude can do. Some are built-in and others are personal skills you install or create.
questions.section: How Claude Works Under the Hood

questions.definition: Create, Read, Update, Delete. These are the four basic operations you can perform on a file.
A lightweight text format that uses simple symbols like # for headings and - for lists to add structure. It is human-readable and widely supported.
A plain-text file where data is separated by commas. It is essentially a spreadsheet without formatting, tabs, or formulas.
Microsoft Excel's file format. It supports tabs, colors, formulas, and formatting.
Microsoft Word's file format. It supports rich text, tables, and fillable forms.
Microsoft PowerPoint's file format.
A fixed-layout document format. Claude can create and read PDFs, but editing an existing one is more like rebuilding it from scratch.
An image format defined by math rather than pixels, so it scales without losing quality.
A structured data format using key-value pairs. It is commonly used to pass information between systems.
The standard language for structuring web pages.
A block of metadata at the top of a markdown file, written in YAML and enclosed between --- lines. Apps can read it automatically.
A human-readable data format often used for frontmatter and configuration files. It is easy to read but sensitive to indentation and typos.
A defined structure that lists every piece of information an app needs to track and what type each piece is.
The language that controls the visual appearance of web pages, including colors, fonts, spacing, and layout.
The programming language that makes web pages interactive.
A JavaScript framework for building user interfaces out of reusable components.
Pre-built code that your app relies on. When a library fails to load, the features that depend on it break.
A network of servers that hosts common libraries so your app can load them from the internet rather than bundling them locally.
A browser feature that lets a web page save data on your computer so it survives page refreshes and browser restarts.
A browser feature that lets a web page ask you to pick a file from your computer, read it, and work with it without uploading anything to the internet.
The developer panel in a web browser where errors, warnings, and diagnostic messages appear.
In the console, an error means something is broken and will not function. A warning means something is suboptimal but not necessarily broken.
The underlying reason something failed, as opposed to the symptom you see on screen.
A line at the top of a code file that pulls in a library. When an import fails, everything that depends on that library breaks.
Data written directly into the code rather than entered by the user or loaded from a file.
The simplest version of an app that works and is useful.
A collaborative style of development where a human describes what they want in plain language and AI writes the code, with both iterating together in real time.
Clicking directly on displayed text to edit it in place instead of opening a separate edit screen.
The app saves your changes automatically whenever you make them, without needing a Save button.
The process of checking that output meets expectations.
A file that acts as a shortcut to another location on your computer.
Your user folder on macOS, such as /Users/yourname/. Cowork restricts workspaces to paths inside this directory.
A plain-text file for storing secrets like API keys and passwords so they are not written directly into code.
A unique string that grants your app access to an external service and should be stored in an .env file or equivalent, not directly in code.
